data.title: Ichor: Semiconductor Turnaround Continues With Significant Upside
data.description:
Ichor Holdings (ICHR) has been a historically strong performer in the semiconductor wafer fabrication market. Ichor is a small cap player in the gas panel system business currently at $1.66 Billion market cap, an essential partner for modern Fabs with increasing complexity. However, in the previous semiconductor cycle the company lagged. Things may be beginning to change, with industry stalwart ASML calling out improvements in 2026 expectations. Market participants are beginning to expect a broadening of semiconductor demand into 2026 which should benefit Ichor as their biggest customers are the two giants in the space - Lam Research (LRCX) and Applied Materials (AMAT).

The company has lagged its larger peers but this could be due for a snapback in the coming year if the semiconductor bull continues. I last wrote about Ichor way back in 2017 seen here when it was on a run of significant acquisitions. This has given ICHR a strong position in the creation of many components but ultimately the company hasn't been able to capitalize to grow market share. The stock has only increased 70% since, trailing the market significantly as contract manufacturers have struggled to increase importance. Now things may be beginning to change as the industry is in need of supply and capacity ICHR can provide. Signs are beginning to form that Lam Research and Applied Materials have drawn down inventory. That could prove bullish for the companies prospects as revenue flows down the supply chain to the contract manufacturers like ICHR. The company has been staking concrete steps to improve margins after a long period of underperformance, giving it a potential valuation catchup throughout 2026. Recent Q4 results have shined a light on the traction management has gained of late, with the Iranian war pullback giving an excellent entry for new buyers in the shares.

Solid Q4 a sign of things to come

Ichor has been a weak performer in the space over the past 10 years, with a solid niche in gas panels hampered by poor execution. The company has struggled to improve its market share and has a multitude of suppliers that are preventing it from achieving solid gross margins and operating profit. However, the team at ICHR is starting to fix these issues by increasing the amount of ICHR content in its products. As the company adds its own valves, filters, fittings and flow controllers it will improve its margin profile by keeping that profit in house. This should lead to better margins over time, with this being a multi-year story of turnaround. It's main customers are finally back to strong forecasts, which bodes well for ICHR as it works to improve its own standing while supplying significantly more product in 2026. Improvement in gross margin from 11% to 15% by the end of this year should set the company up for solid earnings in 2027 with continued strength in WFE. AI applications are improving the outlook as gate-all-around and advanced packaging are supporting strong growth for the next 2 years or more. This is increasing etch and deposition intensity leading to a strong upcycle for equipment players like Ichor. Visibility our more than 1 year now is strong which will help support multiples this cycle at a higher level than some in recent history. Ichor had $223.6 million of revenue in the December quarter, showing a strong guide with $250 million at the midpoint for the current quarter. Indications from ICHR as well as industry peers point to lowered inventory levels at their major customers, necessitating increasing purchases in calendar 2026. The company even called out revenue growth in every quarter in 2026 in the Q1 conference call, a nice data point with 2H revenue then expected to be above $250 million per quarter. At only 2.2% operating margin for the year 2025, ICHR has a lot of upside to get back to former operating margin highs of 8%+ in 2018. This is a base case bull scenario, with potential upside from acquisitions and improvements in margin or wafer fabrication growth. The company did call out that they aren't modeling for 18-20% gross margin yet, but they did say significant operating margin leverage is coming as they scale in the next 6-7 quarters.

The balance sheet is solid with $98.3 million in cash on hand, helping fund share repurchases, capacity additions and tuck in acquisitions. ICHR does hold $123m in debt but with positive $6m in cash flow in Q4 improving through 2026 it shouldn't hold the business back. The market is hoping ICHR focuses on its core business right now and it seems any acquisitions may be pushed out until 2027 as a result. They are adding significant capacity for machining this year in Mexico as well as ICHR's largest manufacturer center to date in Malaysia. These low cost jurisdictions should help bring costs down over the long term as well once they get ramped into 2027. Below you can see the improvement is partially due to leverage we are already seeing in revenue. Cost reductions and increasing content of parts made in-house are also significant contributors. Some of these improvements will continue into 2027 as well helping the company get back to a cash generative position.

The technical picture has moderated after the Iran conflict began as the high growth stocks took a significant hit amid the selling. However, this has position ICHR now in a good spot for new money as the medium and long term trends are both strong. The recent move upwards as the company started getting 2026 visibly was quite violent with some sellers coming in after the Iran conflict started. As long as the shares are above the 50 day MA the shares are in a strong upward trend and buying on dips is recommended. Currently the shares are down $8 from highs, allowing for a good entry into shares that have a long period of stagnation since its strong run in 2021. Institutional investors continue to add with 40 institutions adding in 4Q against 31 reducing positions as the investment cycle in semiconductors picks up pace.

Buy Rated - Significant runway for improvement

While the semiconductor cycle has been underway for some time among the favored players, it has only just begun for the contract manufacturers. They have struggled in recent years, but this stronger cycle is going to renew their importance to the overall supply chain. Also, Ichor is making significant improvements fundamentally to their business model after struggling for some time. Some of the good news is priced in now after the past 3 months but the shares still have significant upside as 2027 estimates start to form. Those looking for a small cap option in the semiconductor space would do well to purchase ICHR shares below the recent highs of $55.

data.title: Canadian Solar: Hammered After Earnings, Still A Hold
data.description:
Thesis

Canadian Solar Inc. (CSIQ) is a global company that makes solar products and develops energy storage and solar projects. The last time I reviewed the stock, I rated it as a Hold because profit margins were being squeezed, the company was losing cash, and there were still too many risks in how well they could execute their plans to justify buying into the story.

Since then, CSIQ stock’s been down hard, and today’s not making it better. Be that as it may, after reviewing today’s latest results, I’m maintaining my neutral view. The core challenges remain unresolved, and the path to a sustained recovery is uncertain, but the valuation already reflects much of the downside.

My View on Canadian Solar’s Q4 and FY2025 Results

  • Q4 2025 module shipments: 4.3 GW
  • FY2025 module shipments: 24.3 GW
  • FY2025 energy storage shipments: 7.8 GWh.

The fourth quarter ended what had already been a tough 2025 for Canadian Solar. During the year, the company chose not to focus on selling as many solar modules as possible in a weak global market. Instead, it focused on protecting its profit margins and expanding into other areas to reduce risk. The management team clearly said they focused more on higher-value areas, like energy storage, instead of just increasing the amount of products they shipped. It’s another indication the industry hasn’t fixed its basic problems: too much supply, sliding prices, and a demand recovery that’s been slow and unreliable.

  • Q4 2025 revenue: $1.217B (-20.0% Y/Y) misses by $150M.

With solar module deliveries down both sequentially and year-over-year, total revenue took the predictable hit. At the same time, the energy storage business achieved record shipments for the full year, even though deliveries in the fourth quarter were slightly lower due to construction delays at customer sites.

  • Q4 2025 gross margin: 10.2%
  • Q3 2025 gross margin: 17.2%
  • Q4 2024 gross margin: 14.3%
  • Q4 2025 gross profit: $124M.

Profit margins dropped this quarter. This was mainly due to lower storage volumes, smaller contributions from module sales in North America, delays in selling some projects, and losses in value (impairments) on certain project assets. The drop compared to the previous period was especially large because of those losses in asset value. Earnings fell from both module and project sales compared to last year. And while module prices were higher, they didn’t fully close the gap.

  • Q4 2025 operating expenses: $188M
  • Q4 2025 net income: -$86M.

Meanwhile, operating expenses came down compared to earlier periods, mainly because logistics costs were lower. Business slowed, but the company made sure costs didn’t get the same idea. Even so, it reported a net loss due to lower gross profit, ongoing interest costs from having a high level of debt, and other factors such as changes in working capital.

  • Q4 2025 operating cash flow: -$65M.

Cash flow from operations went negative this quarter, mainly because they put more money into project assets as they kept building out the pipeline. That said, inventory came down a bit, which helped offset some of the drag.

  • FY2025 capex: $962M
  • Cash (end 2025): $1.37B.

And capex stayed high all year since they kept pushing hard on the U.S. manufacturing build-out, although it came in a bit under the original plan. Despite all that, the company has a strong cash balance. This is partly because it issued a convertible bond toward the end of the year, which brought in new funds to help accelerate its domestic production efforts.

  • Total debt: $6.5B.

Debt went up as a result of loans taken out to fund new construction projects. Most of these are non-recourse and tied to individual projects, meaning the company itself isn’t directly responsible if something goes wrong. Nevertheless, because this type of business is capital-intensive, the debt’s still sitting there, and it’s not going to watch itself.

In response to these pressures, management's putting more focus on bringing operations back to the United States. They are doing this by taking direct control of their U.S. business through the CS PowerTech platform, increasing production at the Texas module factory, and moving forward with the Indiana cell facility. At the Indiana site, phase one testing is about to begin. Phase two is still on track.

Companies are leaning more into producing content locally, mostly because government policy doesn’t sit still for long. And as those rules change, it just makes more sense to stay closer to home. Recent laws, such as the OBBBA, have introduced stricter rules about using domestically sourced materials. They've also shortened the time companies have to qualify for certain incentives and added new restrictions on involvement from foreign entities. Ongoing trade investigations and early tariffs, like the high countervailing duties on imports from India, inflates costs and ties supply chains in knots. This is why it makes more sense for companies to build and produce goods locally.

There are still risks in putting this into action. Increasing new cell production is technically difficult and not the easiest thing to deal with. At the same time, in early 2026 there'll likely be shortages of approved cells that aren't made by foreign entities, which could slow down U.S. module shipments and reduce profits.

  • Q1 2026 revenue guidance: $0.9B–$1.1B
  • Q1 2026 gross margin guidance: 13%–15%
  • Q1 2026 module shipments: 2.2–2.4 GW
  • Q1 2026 storage shipments: 1.7–1.9 GWh.

Looking ahead, management says that 2026 will be a transition year. The company expects to deal with changing and unpredictable costs for materials, uncertain government policies, and short-term supply shortages in the United States. These issues should improve later in the year, as increased domestic cell production in the second half of 2026 helps reduce the pressure. Nevertheless, they expect lower demand in the first quarter, mainly because of normal seasonal slowdowns. Most of the shipments during this time will go toward catching up on storage orders that were delayed earlier.

Deep Discount Meets Slowing Recovery

After today’s earnings news, CSIQ's now trading at about $13.50 per share, and this price (on yesterday's data) looks extremely cheap to me. Even before the stock dropped today, it was already priced at a level that made it seem like a real bargain, so assuming this massive selloff holds, these metrics, once adjusted for the closing price, technically should only improve.

Even at $18.52 the shares already looked like a deep bargain: 0.21x trailing sales, 0.44x book, EV at roughly 1.3x sales. Drop the price to $13.50 and those same metrics compress further to about 0.15x sales and 0.32x book while the FAST Graphs model pushes the implied annual return even higher.

Its models suggested a possible 145% yearly return once the company’s earnings return to normal and the expected long-term EPS growth rate of 73% starts to take effect.

There's a problem. Earnings landed only hours ago. That triple-digit implied return was always an aggressive mathematical output. After today’s report it looks way more speculative. In today's transcript, management never endorses or even remotely hints at that kind of compounding.

In the short term, they expect some clear challenges. There isn’t enough supply of solar cells that meet current U.S. rules (non-PFE), rising material costs are cutting into profits, and shipments of solar modules in the U.S. are expected to be a bit lower in the first half of 2026 compared to 2025. They expect things to improve only after their own solar cell production lines in Indiana start running more fully in Q2/Q3. It suggests a slow, uneven recovery. No real case for strong double-digit earnings growth anytime soon.

The only areas of growth they point to are their record $3.6 billion energy storage backlog and the expansion of their manufacturing operations in the United States.

These are real positives, yes. But they also make it clear there are risks, like actually delivering on those projects, managing cash flow, and dealing with uncertain government policies.

Final Takeaway

The contrarian in me almost nudged me to buy here. But all things considered, I'm keeping my Hold rating. The potential upside does look appealing for patient investors if the shift to U.S. manufacturing works out and the energy storage business grows. Even so, there're still significant risks here, including high cash usage, and uncertainty around government policies. But if you strongly believe in bringing solar manufacturing back to the U.S. and are comfortable taking on higher risk, it may make sense to buy small amounts selectively. Should this selloff get heavier, I'll start dipping my toes in the water.

data.title: VEA: Lower Correlations And Cheaper Valuations Make The Case
data.description:
Intro

Developed markets are a sensible way to invest for many reasons. The investor may want exposure to different foreign currencies or they may want to diversify away from their own domestic market due to valuations or geopolitical risks. Or perhaps they want to include an equity that offers lower correlation benefits to improve the risk-adjusted return of a total portfolio, like myself.

In this article, I'm going to cover the correlation benefits by including a developed market fund in a globally diversified portfolio. l will then cover why I prefer VEA to target this asset class. And lastly, I will explain why I believe it's a great time to enter a position or rebalance into you haven't already.

Why Choose Developed Markets?

Since my last coverage of Vanguard Developed Markets Index Fund ETF (VEA), it has performed pretty well against the market. Up roughly 22% versus the S& 500 at 12.90% on a total return basis. Although this is a short window, its nice to see some of the developed market premium show itself even in the face of some headwinds in the volatile oil market and tariffs.

One of the reasons it's held strong is because VEA is very diversified among several different industries and countries. For example, energy only makes up 4-5% so while the impact was felt, it still has other holdings to fall back on. It's also not concentrated like the S&P 500. The top 10 holdings for VEA only make up around 11% of portfolio weight where the market is concentrated nearly 35% due to the magnificent 7. This could mean there is less risk tied to VEA currently, but may also explain a main benefit being correlation , or lack thereof, to the market.

One prime reason someone would want to include developed markets in their portfolio is because this distinct asset class tends to provide some anti-correlation to a domestic fund such as US Large cap. This means it could exhibit periods of outperformance which help produce better risk-adjusted returns. On average, developed market show roughly 0.85 correlation to US Large cap which means it moves in lockstep around 85% with the other 15% moving in different directions. Recently however, developed markets show about 60% correlation. This is because developed markets, as measured by VEA returns are up 3.5% while US Large Cap is down -1.5% YTD.

Furthermore, rolling return spreads show developed markets may be attractive currently. Historically, whenever they underperform against the market, the relative expected returns are more attractive signaling a better time to buy to rebalance a greater weighting towards developed markets.

The Seeking Alpha quant system is also signaling a good time to buy. Reducing correlations, attractive return spreads all point me to favoring developed markets currently.

On top of all this, VEA total valuation is at 17-18 times earnings. Compare this to a Shiller PE in the 40s for the total market and you can see why there are a lot of strong reasons why I believe developed markets are attractive.

Why is VEA a Good Fund to Target Developed Markets?

So this raises the question - if developed markets provide excellent correlation benefits, and are at a seemingly attractive relative valuation, why do I consider VEA to be an excellent fund to access this asset class?

Well in general, most developed funds will target the asset class correctly as measured by correlations to the benchmark - which means the next two main factors of fund selection are the assets under management "AUM" or liquidity, and the fund cost.

VEA has an expense ratio of 0.03% which is the lowest or tied to the lowest for peers via Seeking Alpha. On top of this, $222B in AUM outpaces all peers by a landslide with the rest of the funds hovering between $27B and $71B. In other words, VEA is the most liquid and supported while also being the cheapest.

From a total return basis, VEA posts a 52.31% over the last five years coming in second behind the Schwab International Equity ETF (SCHF) at 53.39%. However from a risk standpoint, VEA has the second largest holdings and the least amount of assets in the top 10.

This means VEA will have more attractive risk-adjusted metrics such as sharpe ratio if these trends continue.

Risks

The main risk here is that the recent drop in correlation doesn’t stick. If developed markets move back into tighter lockstep with U.S. large caps, which has historically been the norm, then a drawdown in U.S. equities would likely pull VEA down with it. I think some reasons why this may not fully revert are if valuation spreads remain wide, with international markets trading at a meaningful discount, which can drive differentiated returns over time. On top of that, sector composition is very different. Developed markets have less exposure to mega-cap tech and more to financials and industrials, so they’re not driven by the same earnings engines. Currency movements can also act as a wedge, especially in periods where the U.S. dollar weakens. So while correlations can and do shift, the current backdrop suggests there are still enough structural differences in play to support some level of diversification going forward.

Why is it a Good Buy Today?

At the end of the day, developed markets are starting to do what you actually want them to do which is to diversify. Correlations have come down, performance has started to pick up, and valuations are meaningfully more attractive than what you’re getting in the U.S. right now. That’s usually the setup you want to see. When an asset class has lagged, gotten cheaper, and sentiment isn’t crowded, forward returns tend to look better and we’re starting to see that play out. VEA is just a really clean way to access it. It’s cheap, extremely liquid, and broadly diversified without taking on unnecessary concentration risk. If you’ve let U.S. equities run and haven’t rebalanced in a while, this is one of those spots where it probably makes sense to start leaning back into developed markets.

As it stands today, I subscribe to a 10% weighting in a globally diversified portfolio to VEA.

data.title: Bank of Canada Admits It Can't 'Save' Bubbles
data.description:
The Bank of Canada press conference yesterday finally admitted facts about Canada’s housing market: “The housing market is looking weaker, and weaker than we had incorporated into our January outlook. So, that’s something we will be looking at when we prepare the April outlook.” Canadian home prices “need to come down” and “housing feeds into our forecast and deliberations on monetary policy.”

In other words, interventionist central banks can help balloon debt and asset bubbles, but they can’t stop them from bursting. Here’s the unvarnished video clip.

As the materials’ bubble continues to implode, silver (-44%) since the end of January and gold (-18%), the TSX has fallen just over 6% since March 2, 2026 and is now flat year to date. Parabolic oil prices are bad for everything.

The masses are learning once again that all the marketing hype about “defensive” sectors is the same high risk in different shiny wrappers. Here’s David Rosenberg this morning:

There are few places to hide. Not in stocks - every sector closed in the red. Not in Energy stocks, even with the spike in crude prices. Not in the most defensive sectors, like Heath Care and Consumer Staples, which actually underperformed the market. Not in gold, silver, Bitcoin, or industrial metals, and that includes once-mighty copper. Even the S&P 500 Defense subsector fell by -0.3% yesterday and has been clocked for losses in seven of the past eight sessions. What does the Iran war have to do with U.S. Health Care Service stocks? Yet this group sagged by more than -1.0% for the second day in a row and is off by nearly -10% in less than two weeks.

For those who love to talk about the resilience of the overall equity market, dig a little deeper and you will see that the regional banks - big lenders for private debt and equity funds - are back in a bear market (down -20% from the early February highs). Consumer Finance stocks are off by -26% (back to where they were last June) and yet nobody from the media asked Jay Powell at yesterday’s press conference how that fits into the “economy is solid” narrative. All the more with General Mills (GIS) and Macy’s (M) having just issued some soggy guidance over the consumer outlook.

On a similar note, see Private Credit’s Investor Exodus Spreads to Consumer Loans:

A fund holding consumer and small-business loans made by companies including Affirm (AFRM) and Block (XYZ) is the latest corner of the private-credit market to come under stress.

Stone Ridge Asset Management told clients in the fund last week that recent redemption requests were so high that it would honor only 11% of the amount investors wanted back, according to an investor update viewed by The Wall Street Journal.

That suggests that investors’ concerns about private credit are broadening. Unlike other private-credit funds that experienced a flight of investors in recent weeks, Stone Ridge’s fund didn’t hold loans to software makers or other corporate sectors that investors fear will be displaced by advances in artificial intelligence.

Cash and short- and medium-term treasuries remain the most attractive options until other asset prices return to investment-grade levels down from the latest speculative mania.

Disclosure: No positions

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Micron's Insane Growth Doesn't Change Our Bearish Thesis
data.description:
We closed out 2025 with a Strong Sell rating on Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) arguing that timing was everything. Ironically, our timing was off, with Micron outperforming the S&P 500 (SP500) by almost 60% since that date. Despite that, and Micron's strong recent fiscal Q2 earnings, the ramp of capex across the board along with a cyclical industry reaffirm our thesis Micron is heavily overvalued.

AI vs Windows

DRAM super cycles have happened before, including with the rise of personal computing.

From 1990-1995, as the personal computer became more ubiquitous, YoY growth approached 100% resulting in 6x growth in the DRAM market. However, in 1996 the market declined substantially, as supply overtook demand changing the dynamics substantially.

It's worth noting that 1996 actually had more PCs supplied than any other year of the 90s. But in a competitive market with high margins, producers substantially ramped supply leading to the oversupply. That's because DRAM operates as a commodity-esque market.

Micron Performance

Now AI demand has been staggering.

Micron's quarterly revenue has tripled YoY and demand continues to remain strong. The company's fiscal Q3 single-quarter revenue guidance exceed FY revenue for every year through FY24. That's not surprising in a commodity market, demand has spiked incredibly and supply takes time to catch up accordingly. Prices continue follow supply and demand.

Micron's HBM4 is in high-volume production for Nvidia (NVDA) Rubin, likely to be the most sold chip of all time. That rating is key for Micron's ability to maintain this revenue and demand in the immediate term, but its competitors have the same rating, which means it doesn't change the oligopoly-esque nature of the business.

Micron Financials

Financially, Micron's performance was strong, but it's also where we start to see some concerns.

The company had $11.9 billion in CFFO in the quarter, 50% of revenue, but capex has ramped up dramatically. The company's capex guidance took FCF down to less than $7 billion or $28 billion annualized. Versus its current massively increased market cap, that's a ~5% FCF yield. That's a poor FCF yield for the boom phase of a commodity company.

For reference, during both the 2010 and 2019 DRAM boom cycles, Micron hit a comfortable double-digit FCF yield (pushing 20%). That makes much more sense in a world where these cycles are 2-3 years long.

Now the expansion isn't done. The company's FQ3-26 guidance of $34 billion in revenue at an 81% gross margin means cash flow will increase ~50% from FQ2-26 levels, implying FCF growing towards $9 billion (capex expected to increase by $2 billion). Annualized that would be a ~7% FCF yield, again not sufficient in our view.

Micron Market Guidance

Those who expect the higher prices to stay here forever are not paying attention to the capex side.

The nature of the commodity business is in an era of incredibly high profits, capex ramps up substantially. That's to preserve market share versus competitors and meet customer demand. In 2024, Micron's capex was approximately $8 billion, and now that's more than tripling to $25 billion in 2026.

The company has guided for 2026 and 2027 both to see high capex and operating expenses.

Competitor Capex

Samsung (SSNLF) and SK hynix, Micron's two primary competitors aren't sitting idle, either.

SK Hynix is expecting more than $30 billion in 2026 capex and double-digit growth in 2027. That's from $11 billion in 2024. Samsung's capex budget is expected to come in ~$36 billion.

U.S. hyperscalers, the largest purchasers of GPUs ramped up their capex in 2026 so this price increase isn't surprising. From 2025-2026 capex plans are seeing double-digit growth to more than $600 billion. However, growth is expected to moderate in 2027-2028 (not surprising given that the hyperscalers are spending all FCF on AI and turning to debt).

That 2027-2028 moderation of growth means moderation of demand which gives the memory producers time to catch up. More so, the end of FCF to invest means we don't see substantial room for capex growth from the debt markets.

The above forecast for HBM capacity shows how quickly wafer starts are being ramped. With the companies able to ramp capacity by 48% YoY and ramping up capex substantially, we see continued capex growth into 2027-2028. With capex expected to flatline from the hyperscaler perspective, we expect prices for HBM to decline.

Major forecasts are that the shortage would remain until 2027, but from there it's expected to go towards normal. That explains why in prior up-cycles Micron has sat at a 20% FCF rate for 2-3 years. Versus the current single-digit FCF rate, which doesn't justify the shareholder returns for a few year cycle.

Importantly, capex expenses are fixed. There's a reason why Micron's FY-23 through FQ-26 shareholder returns, a 5-year period, are roughly $6 billion only. With the company tripling capex, if it keeps capex too low it risks losing market share to competitors, but if it keeps capex too high, it risks negative FCF once prices recover.

Versus a $520 billion market cap, we don't see the risk-reward as there. More so, all of this presumes the AI capex growth and demand stays high. With reports of pullbacks on projects, and the inelastic supply from these new fabs, any downturn could result in a rapid collapse in HBM demand.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is insatiable AI demand. AI capex wasn't expected to almost double from 2025-2026 but it has. We see a lack of FCF as a limitation at this point, but all of the hyperscalers technically do have the balance sheets to take out enormous amounts of debt.

Forecasts are for ramps to slow down in 2027-2028, but if they stay higher for longer, that could result in strong cash flow for Micron for much longer.

Conclusion

Micron has had an incredibly start to the year, and the company's guidance indicates that outperformance is expected to continue. The company's FQ-3 guidance is more revenue in one quarter than any year in its history outside of 2025. Despite that, with Micron already at a more than $500 billion valuation, risks remain in our view.

Specifically the company's FCF remains low versus where it has been in prior bull cycles, concerning given that most forecasts are for the supply to catch up by 2028. Additionally, the company is rapidly ramping capex, locking itself into stronger spending even if the market changes. That would substantially hurt Micron's ability to justify its valuation.

Let us know your thoughts in the comments below.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Oil Market Is Telling Us The Iran War Is Not Ending Soon
data.description:
Brent oil again jumps to nearly $120 on strikes on Gulf facilities

Overnight, Brent crude oil soared approximately 10% to nearly $120 on escalating attacks on major Middle East oil facilities. This includes attacks on Qatar's LNG plant, gas facilities in Abu Dhabi, two oil refineries in Kuwait, and a Saudi refinery in Yanbu that is in the vicinity of the Red Sea. Since the blockage of the Strait of Hormuz, Saudi Arabia has been rerouting oil via a major pipeline to the western port of Yanbu. Until March 17, Saudi crude exports from Yanbu had increased to slightly over 4 mb/d from the pre-war level of approx. 1 mb/d (Chart 1). This means that sizeable supply diversion is already in place. Notwithstanding, the latest attacks raise risks for the production disruption to be steeper than what we have discussed ahead and thereby aggravate the supply shock in the oil market.

Chart 1 – Saudi Arabia's Crude Exports via Yanbu (Red Sea)

Backwardation in crude oil signals continuation of the conflict for the coming 2-3 months

In the past week to 10 days, the Brent futures curve has understandably shifted upwards because of the escalation of the war with Iran. Backwardation steepened in the past week for contracts until September, with no material change in the steepness overnight (Chart 2). This indicates that though oil traders see little likelihood of an immediate resolution. The futures curve is clearly showing signs of flattening from October onwards. It is important to understand that even if a ceasefire is announced today, it will take anywhere between four and eight weeks for the oil facilities and trade routes to be fully back on track, depending on the extent of the damage. Therefore, elevated time spreads and steep backwardation until September are consistent with the market pricing in a conflict for at least the next 2-3 months.

Chart 2 – Brent Forward Curve (current, yesterday vs. last week)

The IEA's release plan implies a minimum 60-day duration of the conflict

A week to 10 days into the Iran War that started on February 28, the production cuts in the Middle East are estimated to be approximately 6.7 mb/d. These are forced curtailments because of the filling up of storage facilities. This is resulting from the choking of exports following the Strait of Hormuz blockage. Saudi Arabia's and Iraq’s cuts are estimated to be 2.5 mb/d and 2.9 mb/d, respectively. At the same time, the UAE and Kuwait have slashed production by 800 kb/d and 500 kb/d, respectively (Chart 3). While the headline crude oil trade interruption is cited to be humongous, i.e., to the tune of 20-21 mb/d. The output decline is to be lower because countries are pursuing alternative routes to circumvent the Strait. Nonetheless, as stated earlier, the latest attacks, especially on the Red Sea, raise the stakes for the outages to be higher.

Chart 3 – Oil Cuts by Middle East Producers

On March 11th, the IEA announced the release of 400 mln bbls of emergency oil reserves. This does not seem to be a random number. In our view, this quantum suggests that IEA believes that the supply curtailments will last for a considerable span of time. There are two reasons for this. Firstly, though the size of the release is significant, it amounts to just over one-fifth of its total reserves (including government-controlled). This gives room for the IEA to make additional releases if the need arises, insinuating expectations of a prolonged conflict. Secondly, the release size of 400 mln bbls in the context of the 6.7 mb/d output loss implies release over a theoretical period of 60 days. This could be termed the implied minimum duration of the conflict. Realistically, the pace of the daily release could be in the ballpark range of 2.5-3.0 mb. This means that at best the average flow can offset less than half of the prevailing Middle East outage. Thus, IEA’s release modality seems to be a carefully thought-out plan to mitigate the supply volatility in the oil market. Here again hinting at expectations of an elongated battle.

Understanding the risk premium in oil

We categorize the premium in the price of oil as war and geopolitical risk. Prior to the start of this latest conflict and through most of 2025, Brent crude was trading in the $60-65 range. Last year during June 2025, Brent spiked to $80 when the mini war with Iran erupted. Prices later fizzled out when the market increasingly focused on the “oversupply” in oil coming from OPEC+ supply additions and limited demand growth. This time oil initially spiked to $80, assuming that this will be a limited-scale conflict with a swift resolution. Prices have since kept on climbing with significant volatility due to uncertainty and intensifications in the war. In this context, Brent crude between the $65-80 range is the geopolitical risk premium. While prices for Brent crude above $80 can be termed as the “war premium.” Assuming production disruption is contained and the war ends in 2-3 months, oil will broadly remain in the $90-110 range. Any weakness should be used to build and consolidate long positions until a definite and firm ceasefire is reached. Given the hostilities of the past few days, any potential truce will be viewed with skepticism and will require successive confidence-building measures to sustain.

data.title: BUI: Recent Correction Looks Like A Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Main Thesis & Background

The purpose of this article is to evaluate the BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure & Power Opportunities Trust (BUI) as an investment option at its current market price. The fund is managed by BlackRock, and its objective is to "provide total return through a combination of current income, current gains, and long-term capital appreciation." BUI does this by investing in equity securities in the Utilities, Infrastructure, and Power sectors and by utilizing an option-writing strategy.

My followers know this is a fund I have recommended many times over the years and have owned for the long run. This sentiment was reiterated back in Q4 when I suggested BUI was a sold buy. Looking back, this was certainly a correct call in hindsight:

This shows that BUI has clearly been a source of "alpha" over time and I have indeed been enjoying the ride. But with the uptick in volatility across the market, I thought now was a good time to take another look at this fund. BUI has certainly had its share of challenges of late but I view it opportunistically. I believe the Utilities sector remains poised to do well and the correction in share price for this fund opens up a buying opportunity in my view. I will explain why in detail below.

From Double-Digit Premium To Discount

One of the primary reasons for my update on BUI right now has to do with valuation. This is a fund I always keep track of and got concerned when the valuation seemed to get stretched well beyond my comfort zone. In fact, just a few weeks ago the premium to NAV touched near 10%! That is unusual for this fund in particular and is generally outside my "buy zone" for any CEF. Suffice to say, those who got cautious at those levels made the right decision. BUI suffered a sharp pullback over the past month or so and I have to figure valuation played a big part in that:

This chart does look a bit ugly but the good news is that those who were patient can now get this CEF at a much more attractive price. And I don't just the mean the obvious in terms of lower market price compared to before. I mean that the fund's valuation has shifted markedly with respect to its NAV. What was once a near double-digit premium a short while ago has turned into a sizable discount today (3/17):

Now I will reiterate this is a fund that often trades at a discount so this doesn't mean one should expect automatic strong returns going forward. But it does emphasize why being smart with timing in all CEFs (not just BUI) can really be advantageous. Those who overpaid a few weeks ago are feeling the pain, but that can be our gain. With a discount in excess of 3%, I am comfortable adding to my position at these levels. This helps to justify the continued "buy" rating that I have on the fund.

The Non-US Exposure Still Entices Me

Expanding on the valuation story, the non-US exposure that BUI has in also central to why I like the fund at present. Everyone knows by now that stocks in the US are expensive. And while the rest of the world may not be "cheap", both non-US developed markets and emerging markets are trading at substantial discounts to the US in aggregate:

As a reminder, this fund has quite a bit of non-US exposure, which is why this is relevant for the review. In fact, BUI has roughly 1/3 of its assets overseas:

This distinction helps to justify why I see BUI as a solid buy here as opposed to other Utilities ETFs. The ones I follow (and own) tend to be US-centric, so BUI provides a nice balance in that regard. Essentially, I see BUI offering value in isolation (due to its discount to NAV) and also in relation to the US market given that non-US equities are offering lower P/E ratios (on average) at the moment. To me this is a win-win and helps support my bullish view for now.

This Remains A Multi-Decade Demand Play

I will now shift the discussion towards the broader Utilities and power sectors. This is obviously crucial when evaluating BUI because this fund tracks those areas. The story has been largely positive over the last few years and BUI has helped to both diversify one's portfolio away from the Tech-heavy S&P 500 and also to provide strong returns. This has given me confidence in the fund as a core holding and the good news is that I don't see this story changing much going forward.

I say that because power demand is not slowing down - nor will it any time soon. In fact, this is a sector of growth that is both consistent and sustainable. This is supported by the fact the power demand has been growing by a reasonable level each decade and that is the forecast for the next thirty years as well:

As you can see, the demand story is one of acceleration for this time being. This is expected to continue due to trends such as electrification (both in the US and Europe especially) and the surge in data center expansion to meet the new needs for AI. This has made Utilities go from "boring" to "exciting" in the investment realm and I'm happy to have been a part of it. Unless something changes in a big way, and I don't see that happening, this will remain a corner of the market retail investors should look to get exposure to.

Income Has Not Been As Reliant On ROC

Looking back at BUI individually, the income stream has been another positive factor for the fund. BUI has been able to pump out a high yield compared to passive ETFs in the sector. At present this is around 6% to investors on a monthly basis:

This is a reasonable income stream, even with more rates cuts unlikely in the short run. Further, the former structure of this CEF compared to others (which use more traditional forms of leverage rather than options writing) means the payout was more stable in my view. While many other CEFs (both in the BlackRock family and outside of it) rely on ROC to meet their payouts, BUI does not to the same degree. In fact, current estimates suggest only 7% of the distribution is from ROC, a figure I view positively:

However, this is where the discussion gets a bit tricky. The reason is that BUI no longer employs an options writing strategy, as noted by the fund late last year in the following press release.

The implication of this is two-fold for me. One is the simple takeaway that BUI is now going to participate more broadly with the upside in the Utilities sector in a way it hasn't been able to before. This is because when the underlying securities performed well, the options would get exercised, meaning that further upside was "called away" and fund owners did not participate. Given my bullish view for this sector, I think this is a good trade-off.

But the trade-off is that income will probably be pressured. I don't see how BUI is going to be able to maintain a 6% yield without either raising its leverage or utilizing more ROC. To me, is a yield is only as good as its sustainability. This puts me at odds with some here on Seeking Alpha but I stand by this view point. And if BUI has to use more ROC, I won't be happy about that.

But, without doing so, the distribution will have to be cut. This will put it more in-line with other passive funds and could make sense, but the market often reacts quite negatively to such cuts in the short-term. I would use any such sell-off as a buying opportunity, but that volatility and/or potential drawdown in price could be more worrisome for some investors. Make sure you understand this and consider your own risk tolerance going forward.

Utility Companies Passing On Costs

My next point is one that could be considered "good" or "bad" based on your perspective. Leaving personal opinions aside, I am going to refer to it as "good" from the point of view of the investor. This is, of course, why we are all here anyway - to invest and make money!

In that vein, the current trend of utility and power providers being able to pass through their rising costs to rate payers is beneficial for investors in this space. As inflation continues to remain sticky, consumers and businesses are feeling the pinch. While rising costs can pressure margins for utility and energy providers as with any other sector, the good news is they have been able to pass along much of these rising costs to their end user. This includes both the residential and commercial consumer. The following graphic will illustrate that electricity prices are climbing at a much faster rate than the broader CPI measure:

What I want to convey is that this is a good sector idea for those that want to stay in front of inflation. By owning the providers of the supply, we can enjoy the rise in costs as these costs flow through (and are ultimately paid by) the end user. I view this as a way of making inflation work for me. Electricity and other power sources are more "needs" than "wants" and that gives the provider more pricing power.

So far regulators have been keen to allow these costs to be passed on and that has provided a nice windfall for the sector. This is showing up for investors in the form of rising share prices (and sustainable dividends) and

The Rights Offering Headwind

The final topic for discussion here is the current rights offering for BUI. This was announced by BlackRock late in February as will allow current shareholders in the fund to purchase more shares and therefore not be diluted by this capital raise (if they choose to). Further, the rights offering will allow those shareholders in question to purchase at a discount to market, as noted in the press release:

Holders of Rights will be entitled to subscribe for additional Shares (the "Offer") at a discount to the market price of the Shares"

In my view this is likely the cause of much of BUI's recent pressure - although the premium to NAV it was trading at is an important culprit as well. But I say this is a headwind because the rights offering can often result in a share price drop. There is a multi-fold rationale for this. One is that rights offerings can be dilutive to the NAV as more shares are issued. If that is the case, NAV drops, and thus so does the share price as investors penalize the fund for doing so.

Two, such an offering can reduce immediate demand because - as the logic will go - why buy more shares now when you can wait and pick them up at a discount in the future? I see the logic there as many may want to sell with the option to buy back later for a lower price or hold their shares and buy-up more on the exercise date, but that limits current demand. As demand dries up in the short run, the share price will suffer, and that is probably what is playing out today.

So why should one be buying in now given this headwind? Well - of course - this depends on your own outlook at risk tolerance. But for me I see much of the worst has passed. The sell-off for BUI has been painful for sure, but I see the big move as having already occurred. This CEF was trading at $30/share earlier this month and now it is under $26.50/share. This 10% correction is probably a bit overblown and, while it could continue, I think this represents the big move and the share price has now stabilized. Further, shareholders can begin to exercise now and that will probably stem the waning demand as current investors want to maintain their ownership percentage.

I will emphasize this going to continue to be more volatile period than usual for the next two weeks. So keep this in mind. There is no need to "rush in" and buy now if this backdrop exceeds your comfort level. I view volatility and uncertainty as a chance for "alpha" - but that often means more risk as well. So weigh this carefully before taking action on BUI at the moment.

Bottom-Line

BUI has been a long-term winner but its recent performance has suffered. This is due to a normalization of the fund's valuation, a broader market pullback, and an upcoming rights offering for current shareholders. But while these headwinds may make some nervous, I view it opportunistically and see this as a reasonable time to buy and/or add to positions. The fund now has a discount to NAV, the rights offering is due to expire in a few weeks, and the Utilities sector more broadly has strong demand tailwinds. For these reasons I think keeping a "buy" rating in place makes sense. I would therefore encourage my followers to give the idea some consideration at this time.

data.title: ServiceTitan: Near-Term Deceleration, But Solid Long-Term Foundation
data.description:
Without a doubt, the most jarring market trend of 2026 is the speed at which enterprise software stocks have crashed to multi-year lows. In the space of just a few months, investors flipped their opinion on AI as a tailwind to business and are now fearing the displacement risk it will have across industries, particularly in enterprise SaaS companies.

ServiceTitan (TTAN), the vertical software company that addresses trades businesses (such as home technicians, electricians, and plumbers), has been among the hardest-hit companies. Since the start of the year, shares of ServiceTitan have lost more than 30% of their value, and relative to highs near $130 notched last May, the stock is down by nearly half. The key question for investors is, is ServiceTitan's decline commensurate with its fundamental performance, or is the selloff overdone?

I last wrote a "Buy" article on ServiceTitan in January, when the stock was trading just shy of $100 per share. Since then, ServiceTitan has lost ~30% of its value. Yes, there are elements that we need to be careful about here: GTV and revenue are decelerating, as ServiceTitan is being held down by a tougher macroeconomy that is sidelining many consumers from home improvements. That said, I think ServiceTitan's lower valuation, plus its consistent margin improvements, helps to compensate us for slower growth. I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating here.

It's useful for us to kick off the discussion with an overview of ServiceTitan and the possibility for AI to take over its core use cases. In short, I think it's unlikely. The chart below is a good refresher on all the core features that ServiceTitan offers to its clients. The company positions itself as a one-stop shop for tradespeople, offering marketing and CRM tools alongside scheduling, dispatch, pricing, and payment offerings.

Yes, it's true that relative to many other SaaS companies, ServiceTitan's customer base skews toward smaller, individual, or regional operators instead of large multinational firms with ingrained software systems. There is a relatively lower switching cost to ripping out ServiceTitan and building homegrown AI solutions, but is it realistic? We find it unlikely that smaller trades businesses would have the expertise, resources, or willingness to try to custom-design DIY their way around the logistical tools that ServiceTitan provides that allow them to focus on their customers. And in many cases, ServiceTitan's technology leads to positive ROI for its customers: in case studies, ServiceTitan cites customers who have been able to reduce staffing levels and boost EBITDA margins due to the company's more efficient dispatching systems.

We note as well that ServiceTitan is also achieving a larger proportion of usage revenue, tying its revenue less to seats and customer counts and allowing the company more room to achieve long-term growth as its customers' reliance on the platform increases. As a reminder, ServiceTitan believes its TAM to be $30+ billion, inclusive of sub-verticals the company currently doesn't service (like heavy construction) - so despite its niche nature, we note that ServiceTitan's annual revenue of just over $1 billion is only about 3%-4% penetration into the overall TAM.

The core concerns that we need to consider are mostly macro-related. High-interest rates and a persistently shaky macro are dampening the housing market and also discouraging home improvements. That said, we also think that we have fair compensation from a valuation perspective to balance out these near-term risks.

At current share prices near $69, ServiceTitan trades at a $6.50 billion market cap. Netting off the $428.8 million of cash (which is now free of debt) on the company's latest balance sheet, ServiceTitan's resulting enterprise value is $6.07 billion.

For FY27 (the year for ServiceTitan ending in January 2027), the company is guiding to $1.11-$1.12 billion in revenue, or 16%-17% y/y growth.

This positions ServiceTitan's valuation at 5.4x EV/FY27 revenue. While ServiceTitan certainly isn't the biggest value deal available in the enterprise software industry at the moment, we note that the company is sitting either in line or cheaper against a number of other mid/high teens revenue growth stocks:

Stay long here and use this year's sharp slide as a buying opportunity.

Q4 Download

Let's now go through ServiceTitan's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

ServiceTitan's revenue grew 21% y/y to $254.0 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $245.5 million (+17% y/y) by a four-point margin. While the beat is impressive, we note that growth did decelerate four points as well versus 21% y/y growth in Q3. Meanwhile, underlying subscription revenue of $192 million grew at a slightly faster 23% y/y pace.

Gross transaction value, which represents the total dollar value of all transactions conducted through ServiceTitan's platform, also showed a substantial six-point deceleration to 16% y/y growth, from 22% y/y growth in Q3.

A larger portion of ServiceTitan's revenue is shifting to usage-based deals. Usage-based revenue grew 22% y/y to $53 million in the quarter, representing 0.27% of GTV (slightly higher y/y). The company continues to bank on usage revenue becoming a larger slice of the pie and a key growth driver going forward. Per CFO Dave Sherry's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

Usage revenue grew 22% year-over-year to $53 million. FinTech utilization remained strong again this period. We also benefited from monetization of our partner ecosystem, which does not directly correlate with GTV and from early growth in Virtual Agents revenue. Looking forward, we believe that growth from these factors could lead Usage revenue to grow more quickly than GTV in FY '27. Total platform revenue for Q4, the sum of Subscription and Usage revenue grew 23% year-over-year to $245 million.

Another positive indicator: net revenue retention rates clocked in at 110% in Q4, indicating that the average customer expanded their spending on ServiceTitan's platform by 10% y/y, net of churning customers. This signals that customers are willing to continue building with ServiceTitan for the long haul and defrays any notion that ServiceTitan can be easily replaced by AI.

On the profitability front, ServiceTitan notes that the larger, higher-spending customer base drove a 360 bps y/y lift in pro forma gross margins to 73.8% (a chunk of the y/y lift was also driven by the company reclassifying certain expenses to the sales and marketing line). Meanwhile, pro forma operating margins rose at a stunning 740 bps y/y pace to 10.7%, from just 3.3% in the year-ago Q4.

Key Takeaways

We, of course, need to be wary of the fact that ServiceTitan is running up against a cooler macroeconomic climate that may be sapping demand for home improvement projects. This is readily observed in the company's slowing revenue and GTV growth rates. At the same time, however, the sudden and sharp crash in the stock overstates what is largely a cynical problem for ServiceTitan. Looking longer term, we think the company is still building a fantastic foundation, with large improvements in operating margin and a shift to capturing more customer spend via usage-based deals.

Take advantage of this dip as a buying opportunity.

data.title: NORW: Profiting From The Energy Crisis, But Risk-Reward Isn't Ideal (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Background

The Global X MSCI Norway ETF (NORW), which has been in existence since 2010 (with AUM aggregating to over $130M) is a passively managed ETF that tracks the MSCI Norway IMI 25/50 Index (which, in turn, is set-up to cover 99% of the entire free-float of the Norwegian equity markets). As things stand, NORW currently covers 56 stocks in total with the largest stake of the portfolio (45%) consisting of Norwegian mid-caps.

Over the last four years, we have written about this product a couple of times before but only turned bullish on it in January 2025. Our more optimistic stance 14 months ago, was driven by a more salubrious macro back drop, cheap valuations, a compelling dividend landscape, and a promising technical backdrop. NORW, has since done pretty well, generating total returns of ~54%, which is almost twice as much as its European peers, and 2.7x more than global stocks.

After a relatively rewarding 14 months, how should investors approach NORW now?

The Ongoing Energy Crisis Bodes Well, But Rate Hikes Could Also Be On The Cards

Norway’s macro picture still looks pretty encouraging. Compared to the tepid real GDP growth (0.6-0.7% p.a) that was seen in 2023/2024, the growth trajectory is now more than double that pace at around the 1.7% levels.

We suspect there could be upside risks to GDP growth this year (potentially closer to 2%), as NORW’s strong export positioning has received another fillip of late. Compared to last year, where exports served as a drag on growth (-1.8%), this year, things were poised to pick up (by 1%), even before the Iran war reared its ugly head. Now however, with restrictions around the Strait of Hormuz, there are signs of a brewing energy crisis in Europe; Norway, which is one of the most energy-rich nations that exports around 88% of its total energy production stands to gain. For further context, note that Norway currently meets 20% of Europe’s oil demand and 30% of its natural gas demand.

Interestingly, the Norwegian energy major – Equinor ASA (EQNR), which is incidentally also the largest holding in NORW’s portfolio (with a weight of over 13%) is currently operating at full capacity, and is well poised to charge premiums on its production (EQNR serves as the largest pipe gas exporter in Europe), which typically works out to around $2 per MMBtu). Think of the margins that could potentially be made across the quarter, when EU prices as of late January were already trending higher at around $12/MMBtu.

Having said that, everything may not necessarily be hunky dory for Norway, as one should also keep in mind the risk of import inflation, and how that could dictate the central bank’s monetary policy decisions. One of the reasons why NORW has done pretty well, is that the Norges Bank has been driving easier monetary conditions with 50bps worth of rate cuts seen since mid-2025 with the rate now at 4%.

However, core inflation over the last couple of months has already been catching the central bank off guard, and it is now expected that rates could well end up back at the 4.5% level by the end of this year.

Dividend Picture Is Not As Stellar As Before

Another supplementary reason for pursuing NORW back in January 2025, was because its yield position looked very alluring. To highlight, while European ETFs (such as the iShares Core MSCI Europe ETF (IEUR)) were yielding decent enough figures of over 3% back then, NORW was offering a yield that was closer to 6%! That narrative has now flipped, with NORW only offering a yield of less than 2.8% (on par with the median ETF yield), which is not only below what IEUR is offering (still maintaining 3% levels), but also a good 100bps below its own historical standards (over the past five years, it has averaged 3.8%).

Admittedly, some of this is a function of the meaningful price appreciation we’ve seen, but also consider that last year’s payouts were quite underwhelming compared to what was seen in 2023 and 2024 (over a 3-year basis, annual dividends have not grown, and relative to last year alone, it has contracted by 27%)

Looking ahead, it is difficult to imagine those yields getting back up to those 6% levels any time soon, and we’re staring at quite a mixed dividend outlook, considering what the top holdings of NORW have in store.

For instance, while NORW’s largest holding- Equinor, saw its most recent dividend hike only come in at 5% YoY, but the company’s ambition is to typically grow their dividends in line with their underlying earnings, and consensus believes that the company could see around 18% earnings growth by the end of this year.

Meanwhile, EQNR is also going to trim its organic CAPEX by $4B over the next couple of years, which should provide further support to free cash flow and the scope of diverting that to shareholder distributions (this may not necessarily go towards dividends and could come via repurchases).

In contrast, the only other large holding with a double-digit percentage weight in NORW- DNB Bank ASA (DNBBY), which has a policy of paying back 50% of its profits, is staring at lower earnings of around 6% YoY, which won’t reflect well on the distributions.

All in all, it's fair to say that, while it certainly isn’t an issue, NORW’s dividend profile is not as attractive as before.

Solid Momentum, But Risk-Reward Looks Unfavorable

Considering the ongoing conditions in the global energy markets, investors won’t be surprised to note that NORW is also seeing very strong momentum of late. On the short-term daily charts, since November, the ETF has seen a pattern of higher-highs and higher-lows and is now trading above all its key moving averages (also a good 20% above its 200DMA). While this set-up may enthuse momentum players, those who like to take a broader perspective of things, ought to pay attention to what the long-term charts are suggesting.

The last time we wrote about NORW, we viewed it as an ideal candidate to attract additional European fund flows, as its relative strength ratio (versus other European markets) was trading at a deep discount to its long-term mean. In the following months, we’ve seen that upward mean reversion trade play out, and now the set-up doesn’t look very encouraging with the current ratio, perched at an 8% premium over its long-term average. Also, the valuation gap between Norwegian stocks and European stocks isn’t as wide as it was back then when NORW was priced at just a 10x earnings multiple. As thing sand, NORW is now priced at over 14x earnings and 1.4x sales, which is not an ocean away from the corresponding multiples of European stocks ( 15.9x earnings and 1.6x sales).

Meanwhile, if we look at NORW’s own long-term charts (the monthly time frame), there are quite a few signs of it coming across as overextended to the upside. Firstly, over the last 14 years, we can see that NORW has progressed higher, but respected the boundaries of an ascending channel (the two black lines). It is now perched right around the upper channel boundary. Secondly, pretty much the entire candle of March 2026 is now priced outside the upper bollinger band (which is already two standard deviations away from the 20-month rolling average). Thirdly, the RSI, indicator has reached overbought levels, which were last seen in 2021, after which there wasn’t a meaningful upward move in the price.

Closing Thoughts

NORW has experienced a solid run since we last covered it, and it still continues to see strong momentum, because of Norway’s strong positioning in the energy export markets. However, conditions on the long-term charts look overextended, and the dividend profile isn’t as alluring as before. We thus think it would be sensible to take some profits now and revert our rating to a hold.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: HeartFlow: Getting Into The Flow
data.description:
Shares of HeartFlow, Inc. (HTFL) have recovered to levels in the mid-twenties following a solid end to the year 2025, as the company provided guidance for 2026 that pleased investors.

While growth rates are set to come down dramatically, the market seems to read the guidance as a conservative one. Investors are pleased about the adoption of new application groups, as well as prospects for gross margin gains, having the potential to deliver on greater operating leverage down the road.

Other, higher conviction ideas, including the areas of technology and medical applications, can be found at Value In Corporate Events.

Maintaining Growth

HearthFlow reported both full-year and fourth quarter sales growth of 40% for the year 2025. This means that full-year sales surpassed $176 million, as fourth-quarter revenues of $49 million reveal a run rate near $200 million.

The company reported a full-year operating loss of $64 million, up minimally from the year before, indicating some relative progress on the margin front. The same goes for fourth-quarter losses of $17.5 million, trending around $70 million each year, equal to about 35% of sales. Adjusted metrics, such as an adjusted EBITDA loss of $44.7 million, improved by a million compared to 2024.

These revenues were generated from an installed base of 1,465 accounts after 340 accounts were added during the year. For the year 2026, the company guided for about 25% growth, seeing revenues to come in between $218 and $222 million. No overall margin guidance has been provided, apart from gross margins, which are seen at 80-81%, compared to 77% reported in 2025. While the losses likely remain substantial, fortunately, the company has a net cash position of around $280 million, as I would expect losses to come in a bit in the coming year.

With some 85 million shares outstanding, these shares trading at $25, the market value of the firm stands at just over $2.1 billion, or just under $1.9 billion if we factor in net cash holdings. This results in a forward sales multiple of 8-9 times for the operating assets.

Some Perspective

HearthFlow focuses on helping to avoid heart-related diseases and deaths, as heart disease is the prevalent cause of death in the US. The product and solution are based on CAD testing, which is a non-invasive test, one that avoids unnecessary testing while helping to improve medical management.

Its 3D model provides actionable insights into plaque volumes, blood flows, and stenosis to overcome the limitations of other non-invasive tests, often giving false positives or negatives. This should provide the new standard of care versus traditional CAD, with the AI platform allowing for detection, diagnosis, management, and treatment.

The company went public at $19 in August, at the time growing sales by around 40% as well, and that has been the real growth cadence throughout 2025. This was aided by some fundamental support, with the FDA clearing its next-generation plaque analysis algorithm in September. With the company actually ending the year with a similar percentage growth rate, I feel comforted that the 2026 guidance might be on the conservative side.

Hints about this might have been indicated on the conference call, with the company calling for first-quarter growth exceeding 30%, with 1-2% sequential growth seen thereafter. The second quarter is considered a bit softer, thanks to seasonal trends, yet more growth should be seen in the second half of the year, as the plague business will see a bigger contribution.

That would be welcome news, with shares now trading off the highs around the $40 mark, as current losses can be maintained for years, even if no absolute decline in operating losses is seen just yet. This might happen in 2026, given a 3-4% anticipated increase in gross margins and leverage throughout the organization.

Large Growth Opportunities

Sales growth anticipated in 2026 is seen at around 25%, with total revenues seen at a midpoint of around $220 million, including a $16 million contribution from plaque revenues, as the 3-4% increase in gross margins would help the bottom line by some $7 million. Moreover, the company upped the mid-term gross margins target by five points to 85% of sales, which helps in creating a roadmap to see operating leverage, even in gross margins.

The company focuses heavily on improving the analysis and innovation part, using AI actively to use its proprietary database, containing well over 160 million CT images. These are ground truth and non-public domain data, making it hard for AI to find its way around that, effectively shielding the business from AI, at least that is what the company claims.

Beyond growth in these core categories, the company sees potential for the technology (or derivatives thereof) to expand into other areas as well. Moreover, underlying traction is even stronger than reported revenue growth, with global cases up 53% to nearly 58,000 in the fourth quarter, all suggesting stronger traction than reported sales growth.

In my view, the company continues to take market share from market incumbents. While the innovative qualities are seen, the softer growth for 2026 doesn't necessarily make me become a buyer yet, even if some margin gains are seen.

I am still performing a balancing act, seeing the potential of disruptive technology. However, still elevated sales multiples, a slower pace of growth, and substantial losses make me a bit cautious here, awaiting more clues as the year progresses.
